items.title: From Kubernetes Dashboard to Headlamp: Understanding the Transition
items.summary: <p>For many people, Kubernetes Dashboard was their first window into Kubernetes. It offered a simple visual way to see what was running in a cluster, inspect resources, and build confidence without relying on the command line. For years, it helped developers, students, and operators make sense of
items.reason: Kubernetes Dashboard → Headlamp transition is a notable UI evolution for cluster management; no existing wiki page covers this shift.

items.title: Reconciling the Past: Correcting Records for Unfixed Kubernetes CVEs
items.summary:
<p>The Kubernetes project relies on transparency to empower cluster administrators and security
researchers. One important way we do that is by publishing CVE records into the Common
Vulnerabilities and Exposures database. As part of our ongoing effort to mature the official
<a href="https://k
items.reason: Discussion of how Kubernetes records and corrects CVE data is important for security‑focused documentation; the wiki lacks a dedicated article.

items.title: Hardened Images Explained: Fewer CVEs, Smaller Attack Surface
items.summary: When security teams scan their container environments for the first time, they often discover hundreds of known vulnerabilities, and almost none of them trace back to application code. The overwhelming majority come from packages that shipped with the base image: shells, compilers, debug utilities,
items.reason: Hardening container base images is a core topic in container security; a new page on “Hardened Images” would fill a gap.

items.title: What is Software Supply Chain Security?
items.summary: Software supply chain attacks have accelerated faster than most security teams anticipated. Sonatype's 2026 State of the Software Supply Chain report identified more than 454,000 new malicious packages published to open source repositories in 2025, bringing the cumulative total to over 1.2 million s
items.reason: Software supply‑chain security is a high‑impact trend; the wiki currently has no overview of the concept.

items.title: How to Secure AI Agents: A Practical Overview for Development Teams
items.summary: In our State of Agentic AI report, 45% of organizations said they struggle to ensure the tools their agents use are secure and enterprise-ready. That number reflects a broader reality: AI agents are moving into production faster than the security practices around them are maturing. The challenge is
items.reason: Securing AI agents is an emerging security domain; a dedicated article would complement existing AI‑related content.

items.title: What is Sandbox Security?
items.summary: If you're already familiar with sandboxing as an isolation technique, sandbox security is the next layer: the policies, controls, and enforcement mechanisms that make sure those isolation boundaries actually hold under real-world pressure. According to our State of Agentic AI report, 40% of responde
items.reason: Sandbox security expands on isolation techniques; the wiki lacks a focused discussion.

items.title: Coding Agent Horror Stories: The rm -rf ~/ Incident
items.summary: This is Part 2 of our AI Coding Agent Horror Stories series, an in-depth look at real-world security incidents exposing the vulnerabilities in AI coding agents, and how Docker Sandboxes deliver workspace-scoped isolation that contains the worst failures at the execution layer. In part 1 of this seri
items.reason: Real‑world incident case studies for AI coding agents provide valuable lessons; no comparable entry exists.

items.title: GitHub Universe is back: All together now, in the agentic era
items.summary:
GitHub Universe is back: returning to the historic Fort Mason Center in San Francisco on October 28–29, 2026.
The post GitHub Universe is back: All together now, in the agentic era appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Event announcement (GitHub Universe) is time‑specific and not of lasting encyclopedic value.

items.title: GitHub Copilot app: The agent-native desktop experience
items.summary:
At Microsoft Build 2026, GitHub introduced new tools, updates, and surfaces so agents can work the way you already work.
The post GitHub Copilot app: The agent-native desktop experience appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: The GitHub Copilot desktop app introduces a new agent‑native experience; worth documenting as a product overview.

items.title: Still a developer. Just outside. Our latest GitHub Shop collection is here.
items.summary:
The ESC collection lets you escape the confines of your desk and get out into the sun where good ideas are bound to happen.
The post Still a developer. Just outside. Our latest GitHub Shop collection is here. appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Merchandise promotion (GitHub Shop) is commercial fluff, not relevant to technical wiki content.

items.summary:
Discover how to use VS Code to interact with GitHub and maintain your projects.
The post GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with Git and GitHub in VS Code appeared first on The GitHub Blog.

items.reason: Release announcement for Node.js 26.3.0 is a routine version bump; unless major features are highlighted, it does not merit a separate wiki page.

items.title: facebook/react v19.2.7
items.summary:
## React Server Components

- Fixed missing `FormData` entries in Server Actions which regressed in 19.2.6
 ([#36566](https://github.com/facebook/react/pull/36566) by @unstubbable)
items.reason: React 19.2.7 fixes for Server Components and Server Actions are notable; a changelog summary page for React 19.x would be useful.
